rf_banner_hire_follow: We'll turn the noise into signal.
source_material_notice: Source data and documents (agency names, MOAs, official records) remain in English as originally published.
news_heading: News coverage of ICE's 287(g) program in {state}

states_index_subtitle: News summaries and topline figures for all {count} states and territories. Expand any state to read its full summary.
states_index_meta_description: News summaries, maps, and topline figures for ICE's 287(g) immigration enforcement agreements in all {count} U.S. states and territories.
state_meta_description: {count} agencies in {state} participating in the 287(g) immigration enforcement program.
state_no_agencies_body: No law enforcement agency in {state} currently has a signed 287(g) agreement with ICE.
home_meta_description_with_count: {count} local law enforcement agencies have signed 287(g) agreements with ICE, authorizing officers to enforce federal immigration law. We tracked all of them.
home_meta_description_no_data: Tracking local law enforcement agencies with 287(g) agreements authorizing officers to enforce federal immigration law.
home_hero_lead: Under Section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, local police agencies can sign agreements with ICE that authorize their officers to perform immigration enforcement. The program has expanded dramatically since 2025.
home_stat_agencies_footnote: *Unique FBI identifiers; some identifiers have multiple agency names.
home_hero_state_callout_standard: In {state}, {agency_pct}% of agencies are participating, covering {pop_pct}% of the state population.
home_hero_state_callout_fl: In {state}, {agency_pct}% of agencies are participating, covering {pop_pct}% of the state population. Florida mandates 287(g) cooperation under SB 168 (2019).
home_hero_state_callout_none: No agencies in {state} currently participate in 287(g).
home_map_subhead: Click any dot to view the agency's agreement and records.
home_map_size_note: Dot size scales with sworn officer count (FBI LEE).
home_map_caveat: The timeline follows ICE's 287(g) data since December, 2024, the last report before Trump took office. Dots appear as agreements based on their signing date and fade out when an agency drops off ICE's roster. So far, we have not obtained the data released by ICE from January to March 4, 2025. If agencies signed on and left the program during that period, our data will not reflect them.
home_map_statewide_note: {count} statewide agencies aren't shown on the map.
home_map_boundaries_note: Dots mark where an agency is based, not the area it covers. Jurisdiction boundaries are in the works.
home_map_empty_subtitle: Run the pipeline to generate agency data.
home_models_subhead: The three 287(g) program models grant different scopes of authority to local officers.
model_jem_short: If officers suspect a person is illegally in the country and is detained, they can check databases or interview them about their citizenship.
model_jem_detail: Participating officers may issue civil detainers — requests for jails to hold people beyond their scheduled release so ICE can take custody. JEM operates inside detention facilities only, not in the community. It is the oldest and most common 287(g) model.
model_tfm_short: This is the broadest authorization. Officers work alongside ICE agents in the community to make immigration arrests.
model_tfm_detail: TFM grants the broadest authority of the three models. Participating officers can stop, question, and arrest individuals in the community — not only those already in custody. They operate jointly with ICE field agents on targeted enforcement operations.
model_wso_short: This is the most limited model. Officers execute ICE administrative warrants on specific named individuals already incarcerated or detained.
model_wso_detail: Introduced around 2020, the WSO model is narrower than JEM or TFM. Officers may only serve administrative warrants on specific individuals ICE has already identified for removal. They cannot initiate independent enforcement or conduct community operations.
home_search_baseline: {rows} rows across {agencies} agencies in {states} states & territories
trend_subtitle_scoped: Active 287(g) agreements by model in {scope}, since Dec 2024.
trend_subtitle_plain: Active 287(g) agreements by model, since Dec 2024.
trend_aria_label: Active 287(g) agreements by model since December 2024
trend_experimental_note: Experimental (#162). Each line counts active agency–model agreements, so an agency with two models counts once per model. Changes are dated by when they appear in ICE's published list; the Dec 2024 level carries everything signed before then, and the archive has no snapshots between mid-Dec 2024 and early Mar 2025, so the lines run flat there.
about_meta_description: About this project and how to get involved.
about_intro_1_after_name: is a public interest journalism project documenting local law enforcement agencies that have partnered with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement under Section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act.
about_intro_2: The 287(g) program allows ICE to delegate immigration enforcement authority to state and local agencies through a formal Memorandum of Agreement. Officers at participating agencies can be trained and authorized to perform immigration enforcement functions — including identifying, processing, and detaining people for removal.
about_why_1: 287(g) Watch builds on an ecosystem of excellent work tracking ICE and 287(g) agreements. Our goal is to make this data easy for people to access and act on, to be visible to search bots and AI, and to produce powerful visuals. We will continue to enrich the tool with deeper history, contact information, and key context from sources on the ground.
about_involve_lead: Know something about an agency? Have records? See an error? We want to hear from you.
about_involve_records: File public records requests to surface local data
methodology_meta_description: Data sources, limitations, and methods used in this project.
methodology_sources_lead: Agency data is compiled from the following sources:
methodology_source_ice_body: — the current official list of participating agencies published by ICE. Scraped and archived regularly.
methodology_source_appelson_body: — a community archiving effort that maintains historical ICE spreadsheets and individual MOA PDFs going back to 2016.
methodology_source_lee_body: — jurisdiction population, sworn officer and civilian counts, and staffing ratios (officers per 1,000 residents). Each row carries its own data year; we keep the most recent.
methodology_source_wikidata_body: — agency website URLs sourced via SPARQL queries against the Wikidata knowledge graph.
methodology_source_muckrock_body: — linked FOIA requests for individual agencies, sourced from MuckRock's public request database.
methodology_source_census_body: — county, place, and county-subdivision centroid coordinates used to position agencies on the map.
methodology_source_geocodio_body: — a geocoding service used to locate the handful of agencies (university and airport police, regional authorities) the Census files don't recognize by name.
methodology_matching_body: Agency names vary across sources. We normalize names and use fuzzy string matching to link records across datasets, then hand-review ambiguous matches. Each agency in our index includes a confidence score for its cross-source matches.
methodology_mapping_body: The source data gives each agency's state and usually its county, but not a precise address. We place each agency at a representative point: municipal police are mapped to the center of their town or city, and county sheriffs and other county-level agencies to the center of their county, using U.S. Census Bureau gazetteer coordinates. A handful of agencies the Census files don't recognize — university and airport police, a few regional authorities — are located with the Geocod.io geocoding service. These are approximate points showing where an agency is based, not the area it operates in; jurisdiction boundaries are in the works.
methodology_mapping_statewide: Statewide agencies — state police, departments of corrections, and similar bodies — are not shown on the map, because a single dot would misrepresent a jurisdiction that covers the whole state. They remain in the data and in all counts.
methodology_population_heading: A note about jurisdiction and population
methodology_population_body_1: Population jurisdiction has always been a fraught but useful metric in understanding the activities of law enforcement agencies. We include it here to provide a very rough sense of the scale of law enforcement involved in 287(g) agreements, while taking care not to overcount overlapping jurisdictions (e.g. the population of a city is contained by the population of the county).
methodology_population_body_2: Population data is from the FBI's Law Enforcement Employment dataset and reflects each agency's jurisdictional residential population — the residents of the area the agency serves, not necessarily the people encountering immigration enforcement. And jurisdiction size and enforcement scope diverge sharply by agency type and program model: a state highway patrol's jurisdiction is the whole state but its work looks nothing like a small-town police department's, and a county sheriff running a Jail Enforcement Model only touches people who get booked into the jail.
methodology_limitations_li_1: Some agencies have had agreements lapse and rejoin. Historical participation dates may be incomplete.
methodology_limitations_li_2: MOA PDFs are not available for all agencies. We link to the appelson archive where available.
methodology_moa_coverage_body: Agency pages show how many of an agency's agreements we have on file — for example, "2 of 3 models on file" means we have PDFs for two of the three participation models the agency holds. The PDFs come from the appelson/Tracking_287g archive and are not available for every agency or every agreement. We only count a PDF toward coverage if we can reliably identify which model it covers; roughly 176 agencies have at least one PDF we couldn't tag that way, and those aren't included in the count.
methodology_moa_signers_heading: Signer data and points of contact
methodology_moa_signers_body: Where we have a PDF, we extract the signatory names and titles, points of contact, ICE field office assignment, and any addendum details directly from the document using pdftotext. MOAs come in three formats — a current ICE template (used for agreements signed June 2025 and later), an earlier ICE template, and a Task Force Model variant — and we've built a separate parser for each. We filter out section headers and boilerplate before storing any name. Everything we extract links back to the source PDF on the agency's page, so you can check it yourself.
methodology_moa_timeline_body_1: The signing date on an agency's page comes from the MOA itself — we read it off the document. Where an agency has held multiple models, each one is matched to the signing date from its own PDF.
methodology_moa_timeline_body_2: When we don't have a signed PDF for a model, we fall back to the first date ICE's roster listed the agency under that model. We label that fallback "first seen in ICE data" — but only when roster detection lagged the signing date by more than two weeks, meaning ICE listed the agreement later than it was actually signed.
methodology_moa_caveats_li_1: Signing dates are transcribed as written in the document and normalized to a calendar date for display. They're the date on the paper — we haven't independently verified them against any official filing record.
methodology_moa_caveats_li_2: Coverage only counts PDFs we can tag to a model. The roughly 176 agencies with untagged PDFs aren't excluded because we think something is wrong — we just can't confirm which model the document belongs to.
methodology_moa_caveats_li_3: The archive includes a small number of near-duplicate re-uploaded PDFs and a few files with dates in their names we couldn't parse. We err on the side of leaving these out rather than guessing.
methodology_moa_caveats_li_4: Every signatory name, title, contact detail, and date we show is checkable against the source PDF linked on the agency's page.
methodology_ai_body_1: Two things on this site are produced with the help of a large language model. The per-state news roundups are generated from published coverage and the agencies' signed agreements using PromptQL, with links back to the original sources. And some agreement details shown on agency pages — signatory names and titles, points of contact, and budget or population figures — are extracted automatically from scanned agreement PDFs.
methodology_ai_body_2: Automated extraction and summarization can be wrong. A model can misread a scanned document, attribute a figure to the wrong field, or state something with confidence that the source does not support. Treat every automated figure and summary as a lead to verify, not a settled fact — always check it against the linked original agreement or news article before relying on or republishing it. If you spot an error, please tell us.
methodology_news_gather_heading: How the per-state news is gathered
methodology_news_gather_body: For each state the pipeline runs a statewide sweep that pulls candidate coverage from two complementary sources, then screens it. The two are paired on purpose, because they miss different things: Google News indexes a narrow, SEO-invested palette of mainstream outlets, while Perplexity casts a wider net across the open web — Substack, blogs, podcasts, and independent creators, where the long-tail, small-agency stories tend to live. Results from both are merged and de-duplicated by headline before anything else happens.
methodology_news_perplexity_body: Perplexity (the sonar model) is queried with three angle prompts per state — one for hard news, one for online discourse (social posts, Substack, podcasts, and creator commentary), and one for legal actions (lawsuits, filings, and advocacy challenges). Scoping every prompt to a single state, rather than one national query, is deliberate: it forces much deeper recall, since an open-ended national prompt only surfaces the famous handful of stories. The three prompts, verbatim:
methodology_news_perplexity_note: Each call carries a fixed system instruction to stay factual, cite sources, and prefer recent reporting that names specific agencies and counties. The cost is trivial — roughly half a cent per call, about two cents per state for all three angles.
methodology_news_google_body: In parallel, the state is swept through the free Google News RSS search endpoint, bounded to the same date window, using six query variants to widen coverage:
methodology_news_google_note: Google News returns opaque, redirect-style article links rather than clean publisher URLs. These are decoded downstream in a residential build, because they don't resolve from a datacenter IP.
methodology_news_relevance_body: The merged candidates pass through a language-model relevance screen that reads each item's headline, publication, and snippet and decides whether it is genuinely about the 287(g) program or local cooperation with ICE. The same pass extracts which agencies and counties an article is actually about, under strict rules: only sheriff's offices, police departments, state police and highway patrol, and jails or corrections that are a named subject of the story count — city councils, commissions, legislatures, mayors, governors, courts, and advocacy groups are excluded, and the list is left empty when uncertain rather than guessed. This is what filters out the classic false positives: wrong-state matches (Christian County, Missouri versus Christian County, Kentucky), weather stories about an 'ice storm,' and unrelated crime coverage. Only articles marked relevant feed the summary.
methodology_news_grounding_body: News is treated as a point-in-time snapshot that can be stale or simply wrong, so it is never trusted on its own. Before any summary is written, the pipeline downloads ICE's current participating-agency roster, filters it to the state, and computes the authoritative agency count and county coverage. The narrative is anchored to that roster: where a story's claim conflicts with it, the roster wins, and program 'growth' is framed only as what the roster shows.
methodology_news_legislation_body: Each state also gets a statewide legislative posture — whether state law compels 287(g) participation, prohibits it, or leaves the choice to local agencies. The classifier is deliberately anchored: it defaults to no statewide law, moves to compels or prohibits only for a concrete, enacted, in-force statewide instrument (a statute, a governor's executive order, or established policy), keys off the substance of a law rather than whether it literally names '287(g),' and never changes a stance without a statutory basis in the text. A standing quality check then re-audits every state's stance against its own description — a text-only contradiction scan — to catch mislabels, such as an enacted anti-sanctuary law that the summary describes but that was wrongly scored as having no statewide law.
methodology_news_output_body: The result is written in plain, news-style prose — a two-sentence summary up top, with events and agencies as the subject — carries inline source links, and is produced in both English and Spanish from an identical set of links.
methodology_updates_body: The pipeline is re-run regularly to capture new ICE announcements. The data snapshot date is shown on each page.
methodology_license_body: Data compiled by this project is made available under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) license. If you use this data, please attribute: Recovered Factory / 287(g) Watch (287g.recoveredfactory.net).
attribution_body: 287(g) Watch is a project of Recovered Factory, built by Tory Lysik and David Eads.
glossary_meta_description: Definitions of key terms used in 287(g) immigration enforcement reporting.
glossary_subtitle: Key terms used in reporting on the 287(g) program and immigration enforcement.
agency_meta_description_base: {agency_name} in {state} has a 287(g) agreement with ICE.
agency_ended_body: {agency_name} no longer appears in ICE's published 287(g) data; it was last listed on {date}. ICE doesn't announce departures, so this reflects removal from the public roster rather than a formal termination.
agency_history_intro: Dated by when each agreement was signed; where ICE's public data lagged more than two weeks behind, the first-seen date is noted too. Changes we can't tie to a signed document are dated by when they were detected.
agency_agreements_caption: MOAs are not available for every agreement.
agency_source_moa: As reported in the agency's MOA
agency_moa_body: The full agreement between {agency_name} and ICE is available below.
agency_contact_none: Contact information for this agency is not yet available.
agency_records_intro: The 287(g) participation data doesn't tell us anything about what happened after the agreement was signed. To learn more, the public will need to ask or formally request information. MuckRock is organizing public records requests about immigration enforcement nationwide:
agency_records_matched_intro: A public records request has already been filed on this agency:
agency_records_guide_label: How to follow the paper trail of ICE's local immigration enforcement
agency_records_diy_cta: Sign up with MuckRock to file your own request
home_map_use_cta: Download our free videos and images →
usemap_meta_title: Use our 287(g) map — free to share | 287(g) Watch
usemap_meta_description: Download the 287(g) Watch map of every active 287(g) agreement as a video, GIF, or static image and use it for free under a Creative Commons license.
usemap_intro: Our map of 287(g) agreements is free for you to use and share as a video, GIF, or static image. Just credit us.
usemap_video_label: Animated map of active 287(g) agreements growing from January 2025 to the present
usemap_square_note: The square clip — the map filling out from January 2025 to today. Good for feeds and embeds.
usemap_vertical_note: A 9:16 cut that pairs the map with the national trend line — sized for Stories, Reels, Shorts, and TikTok.
usemap_vertical_video_label: Vertical animation pairing the 287(g) map with the national trend line, growing from December 2024 to the present
usemap_download_note: The MP4 is best for video editors and social posts; the GIF is best for quick embeds and messaging.
usemap_aspect_note: This is presented in the natural aspect ratio of the content — it's up to you to frame it in your videos however you like.
usemap_license_body_suffix: (Attribution). You're free to share, adapt, and reuse it, including commercially, as long as you credit 287(g) Watch — ideally with a link back to 287g.recoveredfactory.net.
usemap_license_ask: Don't alter the figures. The numbers come straight from our dataset, and changing them would misrepresent the data.
usemap_custom_body_prefix: Want a map of just your state or region, or a white-labeled version?
video_territories_note: plus Guam & the N. Mariana Islands
